message.content: Adversaries may modify code signing policies to execute unsigned or self-signed code, undermining the authenticity and integrity guarantees provided by code signing. This technique can bypass security controls like Driver Signature Enforcement (DSE) on Windows or System Integrity Protection (SIP) on macOS, which typically ensure only valid, signed code runs on a system. Common methods include using command-line or GUI utilities, modifying the registry, rebooting in debug/recovery mode, or altering kernel memory variables. Examples include commands like `bcdedit -set TESTSIGNING ON` on Windows and `csrutil disable` on macOS. Successful modifications may require system reboots and can introduce visible artifacts, which adversaries may attempt to remove. Exploitation for Privilege Escalation using signed but vulnerable drivers is also a tactic. Notable adversaries like APT39, BlackEnergy, Hikit, Pandora, and Turla have employed these methods. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strict code signing policies, monitoring for policy changes, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and respond to unauthorized modifications. Regular audits and integrity checks of critical system configurations can also help in early detection and prevention.
